summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers have a fundamental responsibility to handle application crashes gracefully and systematically. He emphasizes that relying on users to report errors is a failure of professional responsibility, and that automated error logging and reporting should be the first thing set up on any new project. The post distinguishes between mundane validation errors and catastrophic crashes that require different handling strategies. Atwood advocates for public error tracking that motivates teams through shared pain, and leveraging the Pareto principle to fix the most impactful bugs first. His core message is that crashes are inevitable, but ignorance of them is inexcusable.
key_points: Set up an error handling framework as the first thing on any new project - errors are inevitable but ignorance shouldn't be
Never rely on users to report crashes; implement automatic logging and diagnostic reporting to your servers
Replace default system crash screens with custom, helpful error pages that acknowledge fault and suggest workarounds
Maintain a public record of errors visible to the whole team to create shared motivation to fix issues
Apply the 80/20 rule: Microsoft found that fixing 20% of top bugs solved 80% of customer issues
money_quotes: If I've learned anything in the last thirty years, it's that I write shitty software – with bugs.
Errors are inevitable, but ignorance shouldn't be.
It is not the user's job to tell you about errors in your software!
If users have to tell you when your app crashes, and why, you have utterly failed your users.
It's bad enough that the user has to use our crashy software; are we really going to add insult to injury by pressing them into service as QA staff, too?
Most users won't bother telling you about problems. They'll just quietly stop using your application.
Crashes are incredibly annoying to your users. It's only fair that the team behind the software share a little of that pain for each crash.
key_insight: Professional software development means accepting that your code will crash and building comprehensive, automatic error handling from day one rather than burdening users with the responsibility of reporting your failures.
